mnemonics.title: Half Adder Truth Table
mnemonics.content: SUM = A XOR B (different bits give 1), CARRY = A AND B (both 1s give 1). Remember: 'SUM for different, CARRY for same'

mnemonics.title: Full Adder SUM Formula
mnemonics.content: SUM = A ⊕ B ⊕ Cin. Think: 'Two XORs make SUM' — XOR all three inputs together

mnemonics.title: Full Adder CARRY Formula
mnemonics.content: CARRY = (A·B) + (Cin·(A⊕B)). Remember: 'Carry comes from pair or from XOR with carry-in'

mnemonics.title: 4-Bit Adder Propagation
mnemonics.content: Ripple Carry Adder: Each FA's Cout becomes next FA's Cin. Think: 'Ripple flows like water — carry ripples from right to left'

mnemonics.title: BCD Addition Rule
mnemonics.content: If sum > 9 (1001), add 6 (0110) to skip invalid codes. Mnemonic: '9+1=10, add 6 to fix it'

mnemonics.title: Signed Number Overflow
mnemonics.content: Overflow occurs when: Cout of MSB ≠ C_in of MSB. Remember: 'Carry in and carry out must agree for no overflow'

mnemonics.title: 2's Complement Subtraction
mnemonics.content: A - B = A + (2's complement of B). First find B's complement, then add 1, then add to A

acronyms.usage: The core component that performs all arithmetic (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV) and logic operations in a CPU
Basic circuit adding 2 single bits, produces SUM and CARRY (no carry-in)
Adds 3 bits (A, B, Cin) — used to build multi-bit adders by connecting in cascade
Fast adder that calculates carry in advance using Generate (G) and Propagate (P) terms — avoids ripple delay
Represents each decimal digit (0-9) as 4-bit binary. Used in digital displays
Multiplies partial products efficiently by saving carries until final stage

visualTips.visualization: Picture a number line from 0 to 1. Adding 1+0=1 (stay), 1+1=0 with carry (wrap around like a clock hitting 12)
Imagine dominoes falling — each FA's carry-out 'tips' the next FA's carry-in, propagating leftward
Think of a cup (MSB bit). If you pour water (carry) in and it doesn't match what's already there — it overflows!
Picture a number wheel (256 values for 8-bit). Subtracting is just moving backward around the wheel
Imagine a 10-story building (digits 0-9). Adding beyond 9th floor = go to 10th floor — need to 'teleport' by adding 6 to skip invalid floors

analogies.analogy: Like adding two single-digit numbers in primary school — only two numbers, write sum and carry above
Like column addition with a 'carry-in' from previous column — three numbers added together
Like a bucket brigade passing water — each person passes to next, slow but simple
Like a manager who pre-calculates all team members' work before starting — much faster than waiting for each step
Like making change when you don't have exact coins — you borrow from a higher place value
Like a car's odometer rolling over from 999999 to 000000 — the number wraps but the sign changes incorrectly
The ALU is like a calculator's brain — it can add, subtract, multiply (repeated addition), and make comparisons
